message:
I was packing our go-bag last weekend, and my 7-year-old kept asking *why* we needed one. 'But Mom, nothing bad ever happens to us,' she said. And honestly? She's right—nothing *has* happened. That's kind of the point, right?

I realized I was explaining it all wrong. So instead of the scary stuff, I just said, 'You know how we keep a first-aid kit in the bathroom even though we're usually fine? This is like that, but for when we have to leave home fast—like if the power goes out or there's a storm and we want to be ready.'

She nodded. Then she asked if she could pack her stuffed dog in hers. Obviously yes.

I think the best prep talk with kids is the *calm* one—not doom-and-gloom, just practical. 'We have a plan' sounds so much better than 'Something might happen.'

How do you explain preparedness to your kids without freaking them out?
